title: Surface Charge Doping for Ion-Pairing Criticality in Confined Electrolytes
authors: Na Shen, Yabei Wu, Wenqing Zhang
abstract: Dielectric confinement strengthens Coulomb correlations in quasi-two-dimensional electrolytes and can promote Bjerrum pairing in charge-neutral slits. Here we use a generalized Debye-Huckel-Bjerrum theory to show that weak surface charge changes this picture by stoichiometrically doping the slit with mobile counterions. These counterions maintain a finite screening floor, decouple microscopic pairing from macroscopic ionicity, and shift association-driven criticality to lower temperatures. The critical-temperature suppression collapses onto a single scaled perturbation variable, revealing how surface charge and dielectric confinement jointly control charged nanofluidic slits. Brownian-dynamics tests further show that the same counterions are not always fully bulk-like diffusive: at low intrinsic salt density, explicit wall charge slows in-plane diffusion, whereas at higher intrinsic density the wall-induced diffusion penalty decreases and the mobile-counterion description becomes dynamically accurate. These results identify surface charge as a thermodynamic doping field that tunes both correlated ionic stability and the diffusion mechanism in nanofluidic confinement.
summary: This work analyzes how imposing a weak surface charge on confined electrolytes modifies their ionic stability. Using advanced statistical mechanics theory, the authors show that the surface charge acts as a doping agent, preventing complete ion depletion and suppressing the critical temperature for ion pairing. This reveals a universal scaling law governing the interplay between surface chemistry, confinement, and correlated ionic behavior.
main_problem: The study investigates how weak surface charge doping modifies the ion-pairing criticality in quasi-two-dimensional electrolytes, specifically examining the decoupling between microscopic pairing and macroscopic ionicity.
main_result: Surface charge doping suppresses the association-driven thermodynamic instability by maintaining a finite screening floor via mobile counterions, causing the critical temperature to collapse onto a single scaled perturbation variable.
method: The authors employ a generalized Debye-Huckel-Bjerrum theory, supplemented by Brownian-dynamics simulations, to analyze the free energy and stability criteria.
model_or_system: The system is a monovalent 1:1 electrolyte confined in an angstrom-scale slit with planar walls carrying a uniform surface charge. The theory treats surface charge as a thermodynamic doping field influencing the Coulomb correlations.
key_observables: Screening strength ($\kappa_{	ext{eff}}^2$), ionic fractions ($I_{\pm}, I$), critical temperature ($T_c^*$), and in-plane diffusion coefficient ($D_{xy}$).
important_parameters: Surface charge density ($\sigma$), slit width ($h$), intrinsic salt density ($ho_0$), and the dimensionless ratio $\chi = ho_0 / \sigma$.
main_assumptions: The analysis primarily focuses on the weak surface-coupling regime ($h/\lambda_{	ext{GC}} \ll 1$) and assumes the counterions are part of the quasi-2D Coulomb fluid.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the difference between neutral and charged slits regarding ionicity, show the suppression of $T_c^*$ vs. $\sigma$, and demonstrate the collapse of this suppression onto a scaled perturbation variable $X_{\xi}$.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from establishing the physical problem (surface doping effects) to developing the generalized theory, analyzing the resulting decoupling of ion species, and finally validating the theoretical predictions using both scaling laws and explicit Brownian dynamics simulations.
why_interesting: The concept of surface charge acting as a tunable 'thermodynamic doping field' to control phase transitions and transport in confined ionic systems is highly relevant to understanding interfacial phenomena in quantum materials and nanoscale devices.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
